questionText: Calculate the molecular mass of the following: (i) H2O (ii) CO2 (iii) CH4
answer:
Molecular mass of H2O = 2(1.008 amu) + 16.00 amu = 18.016 amu
CO2 = 12.01 amu + 2 x 16.00 amu = 44.01 amu
CH4 = 12.01 amu + 4 (1.008 amu) = 16.042 amu

questionText: Calculate the mass percent of different elements present in sodium sulphate (Na2SO4).
Determine the empirical formula of an oxide of Iron which has 69.9% iron and 30.1% dioxygen by mass.
answer:
Moles of Fe = 69.9/56 = 1.25
Moles of O = 30.1/16 = 1.88
Ratio Fe:O = 1.25:1.88 = 1:1.5 = 2:3
Empirical formula = Fe2O3

questionText: Calculate the amount of carbon dioxide that could be produced when (i) 1 mole of carbon is burnt in air, (ii) 1 mole of carbon is burnt in 16 g of dioxygen, (iii) 2 moles of carbon are burnt in 16 g of dioxygen.
answer:
(i) C + O2 → CO2. 1 mol C gives 1 mol CO2 = 44 g CO2
(ii) 16 g O2 = 0.5 mol O2. O2 is limiting. 0.5 mol CO2 = 22 g CO2
(iii) 16 g O2 = 0.5 mol O2. O2 is limiting. 0.5 mol CO2 = 22 g CO2

questionText: Calculate the mass of sodium acetate (CH3COONa) required to make 500 mL of 0.375 molar aqueous solution. Molar mass of sodium acetate is 82.0245 g mol⁻¹
Calculate the concentration of nitric acid in moles per litre in a sample which has a density 1.41 g mL⁻¹ and the mass percent of nitric acid in it is being 69%.
answer:
Mass of 1 L solution = 1410 g
Mass of HNO3 = 1410 × 0.69 = 972.9 g
Molar mass of HNO3 = 63 g/mol
Moles of HNO3 = 972.9/63 = 15.44 mol
Molarity = 15.44 M

questionText: How much copper can be obtained from 100 g of copper sulphate (CuSO4)? (Atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 amu)
answer:
Molar mass of CuSO4 = 63.5 + 32 + 64 = 159.5 g/mol
Moles of CuSO4 = 100/159.5 = 0.627 mol
Mass of Cu = 0.627 × 63.5 = 39.8 g

questionText: Determine the molecular formula of an oxide of iron in which the mass percent of iron and oxygen are 69.9 and 30.1 respectively. Molar mass of the oxide is 159.8 g mol⁻¹
answer:
Empirical formula = Fe2O3 (same as Q3)
Empirical formula mass = 2(56) + 3(16) = 160 g/mol
n = 159.8/160 ≈ 1
Molecular formula = Fe2O3

questionText: Calculate the atomic mass (average) of chlorine using the following data: Cl-35 (abundance 75.77%, mass 34.9689 u) and Cl-37 (abundance 24.23%, mass 36.9659 u).
In three moles of ethane (C2H6), calculate the following: (i) Number of moles of carbon atoms (ii) Number of moles of hydrogen atoms (iii) Number of molecules of ethane
answer:
(i) Moles of C = 3 × 2 = 6 mol
(ii) Moles of H = 3 × 6 = 18 mol
(iii) Molecules = 3 × 6.022 × 10²³ = 1.806 × 10²⁴ molecules

questionText: What is the concentration of sugar (C12H22O11) in mol L⁻¹ if its 20 g are dissolved in enough water to make a final volume up to 2 L?
If the density of methanol is 0.793 kg L⁻¹, what is its volume needed for making 2.5 L of its 0.25 M solution?
answer:
Moles of methanol needed = 0.25 × 2.5 = 0.625 mol
Molar mass of CH3OH = 32 g/mol
Mass = 0.625 × 32 = 20 g
Volume = 20/793 = 0.02523 L = 25.23 mL

questionText: If mass of air at sea level is 1034 g cm⁻², calculate the pressure in pascal.
answer:
Pressure = force/area = (mass × g)/area
P = 1034 g/cm² × 9.8 m/s² = 1034 × 10⁻³ kg × 9.8 / 10⁻⁴ m²
P = 101332 Pa ≈ 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa

questionText: What is the S.I. unit of mass? How is it defined?
answer:
The SI unit of mass is kilogram (kg).
It is defined as the mass of the international prototype of the kilogram, a platinum-iridium alloy cylinder kept at the International Bureau of Weights and Measures in France.

questionText: Match the following prefixes with their multiples: (i) micro (ii) deca (iii) mega (iv) giga (v) femto
answer:
(i) micro → 10⁻⁶
(ii) deca → 10
(iii) mega → 10⁶
(iv) giga → 10⁹
(v) femto → 10⁻¹⁵

questionText: What do you mean by significant figures?
answer:
Significant figures are the meaningful digits in a measured or calculated quantity. They include all certain digits plus one uncertain (estimated) digit.
Rules: All non-zero digits are significant. Zeros between non-zero digits are significant. Trailing zeros after decimal point are significant. Leading zeros are not significant.

questionText: A sample of drinking water was found to be contaminated with chloroform, CHCl3 at 15 ppm (by mass). (i) Express this in percent by mass (ii) Determine the molality of chloroform in the water sample.
answer:
(i) 15 ppm = 15 mg/kg = 15 × 10⁻⁴ g/100g = 1.5 × 10⁻³ % by mass
(ii) Molar mass of CHCl3 = 119.5 g/mol
Mass of CHCl3 in 1 kg water = 15 × 10⁻³ g
Moles of CHCl3 = 15 × 10⁻³ / 119.5 = 1.255 × 10⁻⁴ mol
Molality = 1.255 × 10⁻⁴ m

questionText: Express the following in scientific notation: (i) 0.0048 (ii) 234,000 (iii) 8008 (iv) 500.0 (v) 6.0012
How many significant figures are present in the following? (i) 0.0025 (ii) 208 (iii) 5005 (iv) 126,000 (v) 500.0 (vi) 2.0034
answer:
(i) 0.0025 → 2 significant figures
(ii) 208 → 3 significant figures
(iii) 5005 → 4 significant figures
(iv) 126,000 → 3 significant figures
(v) 500.0 → 4 significant figures
(vi) 2.0034 → 5 significant figures

questionText: Round up the following upto three significant figures: (i) 34.216 (ii) 10.4107 (iii) 0.04597 (iv) 2808
The following data were obtained when dinitrogen and dioxygen react together to form different compounds. Verify if these data are consistent with the Law of Multiple Proportions.
answer:
Fixing mass of N at 28 g in each compound:
Compound 1: O = 16 g
Compound 2: O = 32 g
Compound 3: O = 48 g
Ratio of O = 16:32:48 = 1:2:3 (simple whole number ratio)
This confirms the Law of Multiple Proportions.

questionText: In the reaction A + B2 → AB2, identify the limiting reagent, if any, in the following reaction mixtures: (i) 300 atoms of A + 200 molecules of B2 (ii) 2 mol A + 3 mol B2 (iii) 100 atoms of A + 100 molecules of B2 (iv) 5 mol A + 2.5 mol B2 (v) 2.5 mol A + 5 mol B2
answer:
(i) Need equal atoms A and molecules B2. 300 A : 200 B2 → B2 is limiting (only 200 AB2 formed)
(ii) Need 1:1 ratio. 2 mol A : 3 mol B2 → A is limiting (2 mol AB2 formed)
(iii) 100 A : 100 B2 → Neither is limiting (stoichiometric amounts)
(iv) 5 mol A : 2.5 mol B2 → B2 is limiting (2.5 mol AB2 formed)
(v) 2.5 mol A : 5 mol B2 → A is limiting (2.5 mol AB2 formed)

questionText: Dinitrogen and dihydrogen react with each other to produce ammonia: N2(g) + 3H2(g) → 2NH3(g). Calculate: (i) the number of moles and mass of ammonia produced when 2000 g of N2 reacts with 1000 g of H2, (ii) will any reactant remain unreacted, (iii) if yes, which one and what would be its mass?
answer:
(i) Moles of N2 = 2000/28 = 71.43 mol; Moles of H2 = 1000/2 = 500 mol
For 71.43 mol N2, need 3 × 71.43 = 214.3 mol H2
H2 available = 500 mol > 214.3 mol, so N2 is limiting
Moles of NH3 = 2 × 71.43 = 142.86 mol
Mass of NH3 = 142.86 × 17 = 2428.6 g
(ii) Yes, H2 remains unreacted
(iii) Excess H2 = 500 - 214.3 = 285.7 mol; Mass = 285.7 × 2 = 571.4 g

questionText: How are 0.50 mol Na2CO3 and 0.50 M Na2CO3 different?
answer:
0.50 mol Na2CO3 refers to the amount of substance (number of moles).
Mass = 0.50 × 106 = 53 g of solid Na2CO3

0.50 M Na2CO3 is a concentration — it means 0.50 mol of Na2CO3 dissolved in 1 litre of solution.
Mass in 1 L = 53 g dissolved in water to make 1 L solution.
They differ in that one is a fixed amount of substance, while the other specifies a concentration.

questionText: If ten volumes of dihydrogen gas reacts with five volumes of dioxygen gas, how many volumes of water vapour could be produced?
answer:
Reaction: 2H2 + O2 → 2H2O
10 vol H2 : 5 vol O2 → ratio is 2:1 (stoichiometric)
Neither is limiting.
10 volumes of H2 produce 10 volumes of H2O (water vapour)
Answer: 10 volumes of water vapour

questionText: Convert the following into basic units: (i) 28.7 pm (ii) 15.15 µs (iii) 25365 mg
Which one of the following will have the largest number of atoms? (i) 1 g Au(s) (ii) 1 g Na(s) (iii) 1 g Li(s) (iv) 1 g Cl2(g)
answer:
Number of atoms = (mass/molar mass) × Avogadro's number
(i) Au: 1/197 × 6.022×10²³ = 3.06×10²¹ atoms
(ii) Na: 1/23 × 6.022×10²³ = 2.62×10²² atoms
(iii) Li: 1/7 × 6.022×10²³ = 8.60×10²² atoms
(iv) Cl2: 1/71 × 6.022×10²³ × 2 = 1.70×10²² atoms
Li has the largest number of atoms.

questionText: Calculate the molarity of a solution of ethanol in water in which the mole fraction of ethanol is 0.040 (assume the density of water to be roughly 1 g mL⁻¹).
answer:
Mole fraction of ethanol = 0.040
In 1 mol of solution: 0.040 mol ethanol + 0.960 mol water
Mass of water = 0.960 × 18 = 17.28 g = 17.28 mL = 0.01728 L
Molarity = 0.040/0.01728 = 2.31 M

questionText: How many significant figures should be present in the answer of the following calculations? (i) 0.02856 × 298.15 × 0.112 / 0.5785 (ii) 5 × 5.364 (iii) 0.0125 + 0.7864 + 0.0215
answer:
(i) Least significant figures in data = 3 (in 0.112) → answer has 3 sig figs
(ii) 5 is exact, 5.364 has 4 sig figs → answer has 4 sig figs
(iii) Addition: answer limited by decimal places. 0.0125 has 4 dp, 0.7864 has 4 dp, 0.0215 has 4 dp → answer has 4 decimal places

questionText: Use the data given in the following table to calculate the molar mass of naturally occurring argon: Ar-36 (0.337%, 35.9675 u), Ar-38 (0.063%, 37.9627 u), Ar-40 (99.600%, 39.9624 u)
Calculate the number of atoms in each of the following: (i) 52 moles of He (ii) 52 u of He (iii) 52 g of He
answer:
(i) 52 mol × 6.022×10²³ = 3.131×10²⁵ atoms
(ii) 52 u / 4 u per atom = 13 atoms
(iii) 52 g / 4 g/mol = 13 mol; 13 × 6.022×10²³ = 7.83×10²⁴ atoms

questionText: A welding fuel gas contains carbon and hydrogen only. Burning a small sample of it in oxygen gives 3.38 g CO2, 0.690 g of H2O and no other products. A volume of 10.0 L of this gas at STP weighs 11.6 g. Find the empirical formula, molar mass and molecular formula.
answer:
Mass of C = 3.38 × 12/44 = 0.9218 g
Mass of H = 0.690 × 2/18 = 0.0767 g
Moles of C = 0.9218/12 = 0.07682
Moles of H = 0.0767/1 = 0.0767
Ratio C:H = 1:1 → Empirical formula = CH
Empirical formula mass = 13 g/mol
10 L at STP = 10/22.4 = 0.4464 mol
Molar mass = 11.6/0.4464 = 25.98 ≈ 26 g/mol
n = 26/13 = 2
Molecular formula = C2H2

questionText: Calcium carbonate reacts with aqueous HCl according to the reaction: CaCO3(s) + 2HCl(aq) → CaCl2(aq) + CO2(g) + H2O(l). What mass of CaCO3 is required to react completely with 25 mL of 0.75 M HCl?

Chlorine is prepared in the laboratory by treating manganese dioxide (MnO2) with aqueous hydrochloric acid according to the reaction: 4HCl(aq) + MnO2(s) → 2H2O(l) + MnCl2(aq) + Cl2(g). How many grams of HCl react with 5.0 g of manganese dioxide?
answer:
Molar mass of MnO2 = 55 + 32 = 87 g/mol
Moles of MnO2 = 5.0/87 = 0.0575 mol
Moles of HCl = 4 × 0.0575 = 0.2299 mol
Mass of HCl = 0.2299 × 36.5 = 8.39 g ≈ 8.4 g
